text: hello and welcome to another session of inguinal hernia and hernia cases today we're going to be talking about management of femoral hernias in women we're going to review a recent case i performed and talk about strategies for effective treatment of femoral hernias this was a thirty nine year old woman with chronic groin discomfort she had a pretty extensive workup which included a full gynecologic workup which demonstrated no abnormalities She was noted to have an infrainguinal bulge with discomfort and pain with activity. An ultrasound showed a bowel containing hernia, likely femoral, with no other surgical history present. We thought she was a good candidate for minimally invasive repair. Femoral hernias present as two point six percent of all groin hernias and are more commonly found in women. They are between twenty two and fifty three percent of all diagnosed inguinal hernias or groin hernias in women, as opposed to one to eight percent for men. Commonly, these are missed at the time of operation, especially when performing an open inguinal repair with a requirement of up to forty percent of patients requiring a re-operation. One third of femoral hernias require emergency surgery. So the recommendations now are to definitively repair them when identified. The gold standard has recently been modified to be MIS or preperitoneal repair.

The surgeon begins developing a peritoneal flap, revealing a femoral hernia on the right and an obturator hernia. The medial ligament appears less prominent, and the peritoneal flap is initiated higher than usual, approximately one to two centimeters above the arcuate line.

The surgeon begins developing the peritoneal flap, focusing on medial landmarks near the pubis without attempting to expose Cooper's ligament due to challenging tissue planes. The dissection progresses towards isolating and reducing the femoral hernia, noting tight tissue planes likely due to inflammation from the chronic incarcerated hernia. The hernia does not contain bowel, but there is visible peritoneal invagination into the space.

The surgeon isolates and identifies the femoral hernia, noting its anatomical relationships: Cooper's ligament posteriorly, lacuna medially, iliopubic tract anteriorly, and the vein laterally. Careful dissection is performed to avoid vascular injury. The obturator hernia is visible and reduced, containing fat with peritoneal invagination. The surgeon proceeds with retropubic dissection.

The surgeon transects the round ligament during a preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair in a female patient, ensuring the mesh can be laid flat. The iliac vessels, including the vein and nerve, are visible as the surgeon develops a pocket for mesh placement.

The surgeon positions the mesh, ensuring it covers well below Cooper's ligament, and fixates it at multiple points on Cooper's ligament to prevent migration. The mesh is placed posteriorly for optimal integration and defect coverage. A standard peritoneal flap closure is performed without any holes in the peritoneum, resulting in a straightforward closure.

Every time I do a flap closure I drop my insufflation pressure down to about six to eight and I try to take my flaps in two bites minimizing any injury or large holes that can happen if you're trying to take big bites. So there you have it, a pretty straightforward femoral and obturator hernia repair. This patient did really well in the post-operative period. She went home the same day and post-operatively had a dramatic improvement in her groin pain. She went from having debilitating pain to essentially zero pain on post-operative day one. No narcotics used and returned to work on post-operative day two. I did the operation on Saturday and she was back to work on Monday as a school teacher. So great case, great outcome and a nice approach to management.
